executive: Real Estate Attorney — 15+ years transaction and litigation practice
summary: Homer's thesis correctly identifies the market opportunity created by NAR settlement unbundling and AI cost compression, but the current legal architecture is fatally incomplete. The platform operates in a zone where every core function carries substantial unauthorized practice of law risk under Florida law, and the 'translation not advice' disclaimer is a legal fiction that will not survive judicial scrutiny.
key_weaknesses: CRITICAL: UPL exposure — contract translation IS legal interpretation under FL law (Florida Bar v. Stupica)
CRITICAL: Translation-not-advice distinction will not survive judicial scrutiny
CRITICAL: Zero completed transactions = zero validated legal architecture
CRITICAL: No E&O insurance = personal founder liability ($15K-$50K/yr needed)
CRITICAL: FL statutory disclosure requirements (Johnson v. Davis) cannot be translated away
key_recommendations: Retain FL Bar-admitted RE counsel for full platform review ($10K-$25K)
Restructure to operate under attorney supervision (not independent)
Never translate/summarize seller disclosures — present verbatim
Restructure negotiation intelligence as data presentation, not recommendations
Run 5-10 shadow transactions alongside traditional agent deals before going live
Consider raising price to $1,499-$2,499 with mandatory attorney review included
Secure E&O insurance before first transaction
deal_breakers: No FL attorney compliance review conducted
Negotiation recommendations = unlicensed brokerage under F.S. 475
key_insight: Translation of legal documents IS the practice of law — Homer must either become a law firm, partner with one, or fundamentally restructure what translation means in its product.
